planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Solomon's seal tolerates deep shade and root competition under trees better than almost any other ornamental perennial.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Variegated Solomon's Seal is the premier ornamental form of the fragrant Solomon's seal — a classic shade garden perennial selected for its handsome, arching stems bearing broad, oval leaves with clean, crisp cream-white margins on green centers. The clean white-variegated foliage makes it one of the brightest, most luminous plants available for deeply shaded positions, reflecting light in the dark garden and combining beautifully with dark-leafed hostas, ferns, and other green-dominated shade companions. In late spring, small, white, pendulous, bell-shaped flowers dangle in pairs from the leaf axils along the underside of each arching stem — delicately fragrant and attractive to pollinators. Dark blue-black berries follow the flowers in summer and fall, providing additional ornamental interest. Polygonatum odoratum spreads slowly by rhizomes to form gradually expanding colonies that naturalize beautifully under deciduous trees, filling the dry shade beneath established trees where many other plants decline. The plant is fully deciduous — going dormant in summer or fall — but the emerging spring shoots are among the most elegant and beautiful of any shade perennial, unfurling like rolled scrolls from the ground. In zone 9, performance varies by summer heat — it typically goes dormant in midsummer heat.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Concord Grape tolerates partial shade well and actually benefits from afternoon shade in zone 9's intense heat, which keeps the foliage cleaner through midsummer.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Concord Grape is one of the finest and most striking modern spiderwort cultivars — a compact, well-branched selection producing abundant clusters of rich grape-purple to deep violet-purple three-petaled flowers with bright yellow stamens from late spring through early summer in zone 9. The deep, saturated purple color is more vivid and consistent than older spiderwort cultivars and is outstanding in combination with chartreuse foliage, silver-leafed plants, and yellow companions. The strap-like, blue-green, grass-like foliage makes it attractive even when not in flower — the foliage texture is distinctive and different from most herbaceous perennials. Like most spiderworts, Concord Grape typically declines and becomes messy in midsummer heat — this is normal; cut the plants back hard (to 6 inches) after the main bloom flush, water well, and fresh new growth and often a secondary bloom flush will occur in fall. In zone 9, afternoon shade prolongs the attractive season and reduces the midsummer decline. Spiderworts spread gradually by self-seeding and rhizome expansion — maintain the colony by dividing every 2–3 years. Excellent in naturalistic woodland edges, rain gardens, and moist low spots where many plants fail.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in late winter or early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; Chattahoochee requires consistent moisture and good organic content in the soil — it does not tolerate drought or heavy clay without amendment.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Chattahoochee phlox is widely considered the finest Phlox divaricata cultivar — a selection originally found growing wild near the Chattahoochee River in Florida, distinguished by its exceptionally clear, lavender-blue to periwinkle-blue flowers with a distinctive deep purple eye at the center of each five-petaled flower. This characteristic purple-eyed blue-lavender color combination is one of the most beautiful in the spring garden, and a mass planting of Chattahoochee phlox in partial shade creates a carpet of color in April through May in zone 9 that rivals any other spring-blooming perennial. The fragrant flowers attract early butterflies and pollinators. After blooming, the semi-evergreen foliage maintains a neat, ground-covering mat through the growing season — Phlox divaricata spreads by stolons and self-seeding to gradually naturalize beneath trees and shrubs. In zone 9, morning sun with afternoon shade is the ideal exposure — the flowers open most fully with some direct light but the plant needs protection from the intense afternoon sun and heat that causes rapid decline. Shear lightly after bloom to neaten and encourage compact new growth. Excellent for woodland gardens, native plant landscapes, and naturalized areas under deciduous shade trees.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Stoplight develops the most vivid red center coloration with some morning light — in deep shade the red becomes less saturated while the chartreuse margin becomes more dominant.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Stoplight is one of the most striking Heucherella cultivars — a hybrid between Heuchera and Tiarella (foam flower) that combines the bold foliage color of the former with the finely lobed leaf shape of the latter. The distinctive leaves display a brilliant red-cherry to crimson central blotch surrounded by a wide chartreuse-yellow to lime-green margin — the red-and-green color combination that gives the variety its 'Stoplight' name. The bold foliage contrast makes Stoplight one of the most visually arresting ground-covering shade plants available, and a group planting creates a striking color combination effect in dark garden corners. In late spring, airy, branching wands of small, white, foam-flower-like blossoms rise above the foliage on delicate stems, adding a light, delicate contrast to the bold leaves. Heucherella cultivars are more heat-tolerant and adaptable than many Heuchera cultivars, performing well through zone 9's summer heat when given adequate moisture and shade from afternoon sun. The slowly spreading clumps fill in over 2–3 seasons and require virtually no maintenance beyond dividing when crowded (every 3–4 years). Excellent in shaded borders, woodland gardens, and as a ground cover under ornamental trees.

planting.note: Plant divisions in late winter or very early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Blue Panda is a cool-season plant in zone 8-9 — plant early enough to allow bloom before summer heat arrives, as the plant goes dormant in hot weather and re-emerges in fall.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Blue Panda is a named selection of Corydalis flexuosa — a Chinese woodland species — producing clusters of unusually vivid, true gentian-blue tubular flowers with a distinctive white eye, mounted on delicate, ferny, blue-green foliage that creates a lacy, fine-textured effect in the spring shade garden. The electric gentian-blue flower color is genuinely rare among herbaceous plants — few perennials produce flowers in this particular clear, saturated blue — making Blue Panda uniquely valuable in the spring shade palette. In zone 9, Blue Panda behaves as a cool-season shade perennial: it emerges in late winter, blooms from March through May, goes dormant as summer heat arrives, and re-emerges in fall for a secondary growth period. This summer dormancy is completely normal and the plant reliably re-emerges the following season. Plant among summer-growing companions (hostas, ferns, astilbes) whose foliage will cover the empty space during summer dormancy. The ferny foliage and the brilliant blue flowers combine beautifully with spring bulbs, hellebores, and other early-season shade companions. In zone 9's warmest locations, summer dormancy may be extended and survival requires excellent drainage to prevent rhizome rot.
